From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japanese manga series by Hisashi Sakaguchi

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Ikkyū | |
| あっかんべェ一休 (*Akkanbe Ikkyū*) | |
| Genre | [Historical fiction](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") |
| Manga | |
| Written by | [Hisashi Sakaguchi](/wiki/Hisashi_Sakaguchi "Hisashi Sakaguchi") |
| Published by | [Kodansha](/wiki/Kodansha "Kodansha") |
| Imprint | Afternoon KC |
| Magazine | *[Monthly Afternoon](/wiki/Monthly_Afternoon "Monthly Afternoon")* |
| Original run | May 25, 1993 – November 25, 1995 |
| Volumes | 4 |

***Ikkyū*** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): あっかんべェ一休, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Akkanbe Ikkyū*; [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") '[Taunting](/wiki/Akanbe "Akanbe") Ikkyū') is a Japanese [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") series written and illustrated by [Hisashi Sakaguchi](/wiki/Hisashi_Sakaguchi "Hisashi Sakaguchi"), based on the life of the 14th century [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") monk [Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB "Ikkyū"). It was serialized in [Kodansha](/wiki/Kodansha "Kodansha")'s [*seinen* manga](/wiki/Seinen_manga "Seinen manga") magazine *[Monthly Afternoon](/wiki/Monthly_Afternoon "Monthly Afternoon")* from May 1993 to November 1995, left unfinished when Sakaguchi died from [acute heart failure](/wiki/Acute_heart_failure "Acute heart failure") at the age of 49.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The manga posthumously received the [Japan Cartoonists Association Award](/wiki/Japan_Cartoonists_Association_Award "Japan Cartoonists Association Award") in 1996.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Set during the tumultuous [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period"), the manga focuses on Ikkyū's spiritual struggles and transformation into an unorthodox, wandering monk. The story combines and truncates many of the facts and fictions of Ikkyū's life and draws influence from [Noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh") theatre.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ikky%C5%AB_(manga)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

See also: [Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB "Ikkyū")

Illegitimately-born son of [Emperor Go-Komatsu](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Komatsu "Emperor Go-Komatsu"), Ikkyū, is given to the Ankokuji Temple in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") for his own safety. Ardent in his studies of [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen"), Ikkyu is a clever boy who becomes tired of the hypocrisy surrounding him at the Ankokuji. Wandering the cities and back-country of Japan, Ikkyu develops a legendary reputation as both an ascetic and libertine monk.

## Publication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ikky%C5%AB_(manga)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Publication")]

Written and illustrated by [Hisashi Sakaguchi](/wiki/Hisashi_Sakaguchi "Hisashi Sakaguchi"), *Ikkyū* was serialized in [Kodansha](/wiki/Kodansha "Kodansha")'s [*seinen* manga](/wiki/Seinen_manga "Seinen manga") magazine *[Monthly Afternoon](/wiki/Monthly_Afternoon "Monthly Afternoon")* from May 25, 1993,[[a]](#cite_note-6) to November 25, 1995,[[b]](#cite_note-9) until his death from [acute heart failure](/wiki/Acute_heart_failure "Acute heart failure") in December 1995. Kodansha collected its chapters in four *[tankōbon](/wiki/Tank%C5%8Dbon "Tankōbon")* volumes, released from December 16, 1993, to January 20, 1996.[[8]](#cite_note-vol1-10)[[6]](#cite_note-vol4-7)

### Volumes

| 2 | June 21, 1994[[9]](#cite_note-11) | [978-4-06-319490-6](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319490-6 "Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319490-6") |
| 3 | April 18, 1995[[10]](#cite_note-12) | [978-4-06-319587-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319587-3 "Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319587-3") |
| 4 | January 20, 1996[[6]](#cite_note-vol4-7) | [978-4-06-319666-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319666-5 "Special:BookSources/978-4-06-319666-5") |

2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** Finished in the magazine's January 1996 issue,[[6]](#cite_note-vol4-7) released on November 25, 1995.[[7]](#cite_note-8)
